---
title: "Optimal Poker Bankroll Management"
author: "Jason Lu"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: true
---

## Introduction

The central question in poker bankroll management is which game to play given a current bankroll. The naive answer — play the game with the highest expected hourly winrate — ignores a key feature of the problem: bankroll is not just a consumption good. It is also a capital good. A larger bankroll unlocks access to higher-stakes games, which offer a higher rate of converting time into money. This means the value of a dollar depends on where it puts you relative to the next move-up threshold.

Because losses are more costly than equal-sized gains — losses delay or foreclose a move up, destroying option value on top of the direct loss — a player with a risk-neutral terminal payoff will behave as if risk-averse in game selection. This risk aversion is not assumed; it is a consequence of the discrete structure of available games.

This document presents two models for deriving optimal game-selection rules. The **finite-horizon DP** solves the problem by backward induction over a fixed time horizon. The **infinite-horizon OBM** finds a stationary policy via value iteration, using the Certainty-Equivalent Winrate (CEW) as its convergence criterion and main output. Both are benchmarked against the **Kelly criterion**.

---

## The Finite-Horizon Model

### Setup

Each period the player holds bankroll $x$ and selects one of $N$ games. Transitions follow a Bernoulli stack-off rule: with probability $p_i = (\sigma_i / B_i)^2$ the player wins or loses a full buyin $B_i$; otherwise the bankroll shifts by the mean gain $\mu_i$. A game is only available when $x \geq B_i + x_{\min}$. The terminal payoff is $V_T(x) = x$ — no curvature, no risk aversion by assumption.

The model is solved by backward induction from the known terminal value, computing the optimal game at each bankroll level for each period $t = T-1, \ldots, 0$.

### Game parameters

| Game | Buyin $B$ | Mean gain $\mu$ ($/hr) | Std dev $\sigma$ ($/hr) |
|------|----------:|----------------------:|------------------------:|
| Work (outside option) | — | 7 | 0 |
| 1/2 NL | 200 | 12 | 80 |
| 2/5 NL | 500 | 20 | 250 |

Work is a risk-free outside option available at any bankroll. The 1/2 and 2/5 games offer higher expected returns but require sufficient bankroll to cover the buyin.

### Optimal policy and value function

The figures below show the optimal game (left) and value function (right) for three periods: $t = 0$ (far from the horizon), $t = 1950$ (50 periods remaining), and $t = 1999$ (final period).

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![Optimal game as a function of bankroll for three periods. Move-up thresholds shift left as the horizon approaches.](figures/finite_dp_policy.png)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![Value function on a log–log scale. The gap below the dashed risk-neutral benchmark V = x reflects the option value of higher stakes.](figures/finite_dp_vf.png)
:::
:::

At $t = 0$, the policy switches from 1/2 to 2/5 at a bankroll of roughly **\$811–\$933**. As the horizon shortens, the threshold falls — with fewer periods remaining, there is less time for the option value of higher stakes to compound, so the player moves up sooner. At $t = 1999$, the player selects the game with the highest raw EV, since no future optionality remains.

### Implied risk aversion

Although the terminal payoff is risk-neutral, the value function is concave in bankroll. The figure below recovers the implied CRRA coefficient $\eta(x)$ by inverting the local ratio of CRRA utilities at consecutive grid points.

![Implied coefficient of relative risk aversion η(x) at t = 0. Reference lines mark log utility (η = 1) and risk neutrality (η = 0).](figures/finite_dp_eta.png)

At low bankrolls, $\eta > 1$ — the option value of the 1/2 game is large relative to current stakes, producing stronger-than-log-utility curvature. At high bankrolls, $\eta$ approaches zero as no binding move-up threshold remains. The curvature comes entirely from the discrete game structure, not from any preference assumption.

---

## The Infinite-Horizon OBM Model

### Setup and the CEW metric

The finite-horizon model requires specifying a time horizon. The infinite-horizon OBM drops this requirement and solves for a stationary policy via value iteration: starting from an initial value function, compute $E[V(x')]$ for each game, update $V$, and repeat until convergence.

Convergence is measured using the **Certainty-Equivalent Winrate (CEW)**. The CEW at bankroll $x$ for game $n$ is the fixed dollar amount per period that leaves the player indifferent between accepting it and playing game $n$ under the current value function. It is a bankroll-adjusted winrate: a high-variance game may have low CEW at small bankrolls but high CEW at large ones. The optimal game at any bankroll is the one with the highest CEW.

### Kelly as a benchmark

The Kelly criterion sets the value function to $\log(x)$ — the player maximises expected log bankroll each period without looking ahead. This has a closed-form solution and requires no iteration. It serves as the one-shot benchmark; the difference between Kelly and OBM CEW curves shows what recursive optionality pricing adds.

### Game parameters

| Game | Buyin $B$ | Winrate $w$ ($/hr) | Std dev $\sigma$ ($/hr) | Min viable bankroll |
|------|----------:|-------------------:|------------------------:|--------------------:|
| Work | — | 5 | 0 | — |
| 1/2 NL | 100 | 10 | 100 | \$300 |
| 2/5 NL | 200 | 15 | 200 | \$600 |
| 5/10 NL | 500 | 20 | 500 | \$1,500 |

A game is available only when the bankroll exceeds twice the buyin and three standard deviations, below which the normal approximation to the session outcome breaks down.

### Convergence

Value iteration converges in 47 iterations, taking under a second. The plot below shows the max relative change in CEW per iteration, starting from iteration 2.

![CEW distance between successive iterations on a log scale. The algorithm crosses the tolerance threshold of 0.01 in about 20 iterations and is flat by iteration 40.](figures/obm_convergence.png)

### CEW curves and thresholds

The figures below show the per-game CEW under OBM (left) and Kelly (right). Dotted horizontals mark true hourly EV; dashed verticals mark move-up thresholds.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![OBM CEW by game. The optimal game at any bankroll is the one with the highest curve.](figures/obm_cew.png)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![Kelly CEW by game. Same format, computed without recursive optionality.](figures/kelly_cew.png)
:::
:::

Both models produce qualitatively similar CEW curves with well-defined crossings that define move-up thresholds. OBM CEW rises more steeply at low bankrolls because the recursive model prices near-term option value more aggressively than Kelly.

### OBM vs Kelly thresholds

![Move-up thresholds under OBM and Kelly on a log scale. OBM recommends moving up earlier at every transition.](figures/obm_vs_kelly_thresholds.png)

| Transition | Kelly threshold | OBM threshold | OBM / Kelly |
|------------|---------------:|--------------:|------------:|
| Work → 1/2 | \$999 | \$546 | 0.55× |
| 1/2 → 2/5  | \$2,994 | \$2,725 | 0.91× |
| 2/5 → 5/10 | \$20,917 | \$18,982 | 0.91× |

OBM recommends moving up earlier at every transition. The gap is largest at the first step (OBM threshold is 55% of Kelly's) because the option value of the 1/2 game is large relative to the outside option. At higher transitions the gap narrows to around 9%, as the marginal option value of the next game is smaller relative to current stakes.

Kelly maximises the current period's bankroll growth rate without accounting for what happens next. OBM prices the value of reaching the next threshold sooner, producing move-up thresholds that are systematically lower.

---

## Conclusion

**Endogenous risk aversion.** A risk-neutral player choosing among discrete games behaves as if risk-averse. The implied CRRA coefficient exceeds log utility at low bankrolls — where option value is large relative to stakes — and declines toward zero as the bankroll grows past all binding thresholds.

**OBM vs Kelly thresholds.** OBM recommends moving up at 55% of the Kelly threshold for the first transition, and around 91% for subsequent transitions. Kelly overstates the required bankroll because it ignores the compounding value of reaching higher games sooner.

Natural extensions include **living expenses** (a per-period withdrawal that makes ruin an absorbing state), **occasional high-stakes invitations** (a higher game available with some probability each period), and **tournament settings** (non-linear prize structures, discrete large buyins, re-entry decisions). Each fits within the dynamic programming framework developed here.
